title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: The board of trustees of a national museum has asked you to produce a report on how the museum can improve its engagement with younger audiences aged 16-25. Your report should identify the main barriers to engagement, assess the effectiveness of current outreach activities, and recommend two or three specific initiatives the museum could implement.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Report on Improving Youth Engagement at the National Museum**

**Introduction**
This report examines the barriers preventing young people aged 16-25 from engaging with the National Museum, evaluates existing outreach provision, and proposes targeted initiatives. Findings are based on visitor data, focus groups conducted with local schools and colleges, and a review of comparable institutions' youth programmes.

**Barriers to Engagement**
Focus group feedback identified three principal barriers. First, young people perceive the museum as presenting a narrow, predominantly Western historical narrative that does not reflect their own cultural identities or communities. Second, the formal, passive nature of most exhibits contrasts unfavourably with the interactive digital experiences to which this age group is accustomed. Third, admission charges, while modest, were identified as a deterrent among participants from lower-income backgrounds.

**Assessment of Current Outreach**
The museum's existing schools programme reaches approximately 4,000 students annually; however, participation is concentrated in primary-age groups, and the programme has minimal reach into further education settings. Social media presence, while active, lacks the co-created content that engages younger audiences most effectively.

**Recommendations**
Three initiatives are proposed: partnering with local colleges to co-develop a youth curatorial programme in which young people design a temporary exhibition; introducing free admission for under-25s on weekday evenings; and commissioning a series of short-form documentary films created by young content producers about items in the collection.

**Conclusion**
Implementing these measures would meaningfully broaden the museum's relevance and accessibility for younger audiences.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt: You have read that a large national museum is planning to return a significant collection of artefacts to their country of origin following a repatriation agreement. Some readers have written to a newspaper arguing that this will impoverish the museum and harm public education. Write a letter to the newspaper expressing your support for the repatriation, addressing the concerns raised by other readers, and explaining the broader benefits of returning cultural property to source communities.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I am writing in response to the letters published on 8th September expressing concern about the National Museum's decision to repatriate the Kelara Collection to its country of origin. While I understand the anxieties raised, I believe the case for repatriation is both ethically compelling and practically sound.

Several correspondents have argued that returning these artefacts will deprive British visitors of access to world-class cultural heritage. This concern, though understandable, rests on a misconception. The repatriation agreement includes a provision for long-term loans, meaning that selected items will continue to be displayed in London on a rotating basis. The educational impact on British audiences will therefore be modest, while the benefit to communities in the country of origin — who have for generations been unable to access objects central to their own cultural identity — will be profound.

More fundamentally, the collection was assembled during a period of colonial occupation under circumstances that would today be considered incompatible with any ethical standard of acquisition. Acknowledging this history is not a threat to museums; it is a precondition for their credibility as institutions committed to truth, justice and the equitable stewardship of human culture.

Rather than viewing repatriation as a loss, we should recognise it as an opportunity: to build genuine international partnerships, to model ethical leadership, and to demonstrate that museums can evolve their relationship with contested collections in ways that serve the common good.

Yours faithfully,
Adeola Mensah

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have recently visited a newly redesigned permanent gallery at a major museum that focuses on the history and culture of a specific region of the world. Write a review for a culture magazine, describing the gallery's design and content, evaluating how effectively it represents the region's history and diverse communities, and recommending whether readers should visit.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**A Long-Overdue Transformation: Review of the New Asia Pacific Gallery**

The National Museum's newly redesigned Asia Pacific Gallery, which opened to the public last month following a four-year, £12 million redevelopment, represents one of the most significant reconfigurations of a permanent national collection in recent memory — and it largely justifies both the investment and the wait.

The gallery's most immediate strength is its visual ambition. The curatorial team has moved decisively away from the glass-case-and-label model, opting instead for immersive room-sized installations that situate objects within the social and ceremonial contexts in which they were originally used. A recreated longhouse interior, constructed in collaboration with communities from Borneo, is particularly effective: visitors encounter objects as living artefacts rather than decontextualised specimens.

The gallery's interpretive text deserves specific praise. Labels are written accessibly and without condescension, and critically, they acknowledge provenance where relevant, including instances where objects were acquired under colonial conditions. This transparency, rare in major public collections, adds considerably to the gallery's intellectual integrity.

If there is a weakness, it lies in the representation of Melanesia and the Pacific Islands, which receives comparatively limited floor space relative to the breadth and complexity of the cultures involved. One hopes this will be addressed in future development phases.

Overall, the Asia Pacific Gallery is a landmark achievement in museum redesign and is warmly recommended. It demonstrates convincingly that permanent collections can be presented in ways that are both academically rigorous and genuinely welcoming to diverse audiences.
